title: Build-Measure-Learn Loop

content.statement: Iterate by building, measuring real behavior, and learning whether to pivot or persevere.
content.question: How fast is your Build-Measure-Learn cycle right now, and what is the next thing slowing it down?
content.equation: Learning Velocity = Cycle Speed × Hypothesis Quality
content.mental_model: Wheel, each turn gets you closer to truth.
content.business_lens: Optimize for cycle speed; treat each cycle as a unit of learning, not just shipping.
content.life_lens: Replace long planning with fast loops of small action and honest measurement.
sop: Repeat, speed up the cycle every iteration
content.explainer: The loop’s power is compounding learning. A team that runs the loop weekly will outlearn a team that runs it monthly. Most startups die because they confuse activity with learning; the loop’s discipline is to extract a real lesson from every cycle.
questions: How fast is your Build-Measure-Learn cycle right now, and what is the next thing slowing it down?
Which stage of your loop, build, measure, or learn, takes the longest, and have you ever actually timed it?
When was the last time a measurement made you change what you were building, rather than just confirming it?
content.equation_example: Rate each 1-10. Cycle Speed 7 (how many loops you complete per month, normalized), Hypothesis Quality 6 (how specific and falsifiable each test was). Score = (7 plus 6) divided by 2 = 6.5. Higher/rising means you're compounding real knowledge fast; lower/falling means you're either moving slowly or testing vague ideas that don't produce a clear answer.
content.closing_statement: Speed of honest feedback beats speed of shipping.

caption:
Principle Nº 288: Build-Measure-Learn Loop

Iterate by building, measuring real behavior, and learning whether to pivot or persevere. The loop’s power is compounding learning.

Ask yourself: How fast is your Build-Measure-Learn cycle right now, and what is the next thing slowing it down?
